elements.sent-2411-001: Decision-making in robotics using denoising diffusion processes has increasingly become a hot research topic, but end-to-end policies perform poorly in tasks with rich contact and have limited controllability.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper proposes Hierarchical Diffusion Policy (HDP), a new imitation learning method of using objective contacts to guide the generation of robot trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-003: The policy is divided into two layers: the high-level policy predicts the contact for the robot's next object manipulation based on 3D information, while the low-level policy predicts the action sequence toward the high-level contact based on the latent variables of observation and contact.
elements.sent-2411-004: We represent both-level policies as conditional denoising diffusion processes, and combine behavioral cloning and Q-learning to optimize the low-level policy for accurately guiding actions towards contact.
elements.sent-2411-005: We benchmark Hierarchical Diffusion Policy across 6 different tasks and find that it significantly outperforms the existing state-of-the-art imitation learning method Diffusion Policy with an average improvement of 20.8\%.
elements.sent-2411-006: We find that contact guidance yields significant improvements, including superior performance, greater interpretability, and stronger controllability, especially on contact-rich tasks.
elements.sent-2411-007: To further unlock the potential of HDP, this paper proposes a set of key technical contributions including snapshot gradient optimization, 3D conditioning, and prompt guidance, which improve the policy's optimization efficiency, spatial awareness, and controllability respectively.
elements.sent-2411-008: Finally, real-world experiments verify that HDP can handle both rigid and deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-009: Code, data and training details can be found at \href{https://github.com/dexin-wang/Hierarchical_Diffusion_Policy {https://github.com/dexin-wang/Hierarchical\_Diffusion\_Policy .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: A learning-based robotic manipulation policy can be represented as a map of observations to optimal actions to accomplish a task.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, compared to other learning problems, predicting robot actions is inherently challenging due to factors such as 3D scene perception, rich contact, and human-robot interaction.
elements.sent-2411-012: Previous researchers attempts to adopt different learning strategies to address this challenge.
elements.sent-2411-013: Reinforcement learning has been widely used to learn robotics tasks since it surpassed humans in playing Atari games \cite{mnih2015human .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The exploration mechanism enables the agent to learn complex operational skills, but also brings low sample efficiency and weak generalization \cite{ladosz2022exploration .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In contrast, imitation learning learns solely from demonstrations, which significantly improves sample efficiency and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-016: However, it struggles to achieve performance beyond that of the expert.
elements.sent-2411-017: Meanwhile, most imitation learning methods struggle to handle multimodal action distributions because they are modeled as unimodal policies \cite{zheng2024imitation .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Recently, diffusion models have attracted attention due to their ability to model complex data distributions \cite{song2020denoising}\cite{nichol2021improved}\cite{rombach2022high .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Some researchers have tried to introduce diffusion models to generate robot actions \cite{wang2022diffusion}\cite{janner2022planning .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Chi et al. proposed Diffusion policy to jointly reason about multi-step action sequences, which achieved significant improvements \cite{chi2023diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Diffusion policy can express multi-modal action distribution and cope with high-dimensional output space while maintaining high sample efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, Diffusion policy performs worse in the contact-rich tasks than in tasks with single contact.
elements.sent-2411-023: We argue that the reason for this phenomenon is that the Diffusion policy does not explicitly model the interaction between the robot and object.
elements.sent-2411-024: In this paper, we introduce contact position to model the interaction between the robot and object.
elements.sent-2411-025: Specifically, we design a new network to predict the objective contact position of the robot and utilize reinforcement learning to guide the policy to generate robot trajectories toward the objective contact.
elements.figure-2411-001: {\includegraphics[scale=0.28]{introduction/introduction.pdf}} \caption{ Inference Process of Hierarchical Diffusion Policy. Compared to the Diffusion Policy that generates operation trajectories end-to-end, HDP introduces objective contacts, predicted by the Guider network or provided by humans, to guide the trajectory generation. } \label{fig_introduction}
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we propose Hierarchical Diffusion Policy (HDP), a novel method that uses contact to guide the generation of robot manipulation trajectories (Fig.~\ref{fig_introduction ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: HDP consists of three networks, namely Guider, Actor, and Critic, which predict objective contact, robot action sequence, and Q-value respectively.
elements.sent-2411-028: The Guider predicts the contact through a conditional denoising diffusion process, which represents the operating position (already in contact) or the contact position to reach the object (not yet in contact).
elements.sent-2411-029: The Actor is also modeled as a conditional denoising diffusion model, which predicts action sequences based on historical observations and objective contacts.
elements.sent-2411-030: We jointly optimize the Actor using behavior cloning and Q-learning algorithms to enable the Actor to establish the guidance of actions by contacts, while learning the conditional probability distribution of actions given observations.
elements.sent-2411-031: By introducing contact guidance for actions, HDP divides the manipulation planning into high-level contact planning and low-level trajectory generation, which brings three advantages:.
elements.sent-2411-032: Short-horizon operation tasks are easier to learn than long-horizon tasks, and the contact condition makes the Actor focus more on short-horizon operation planning.
elements.sent-2411-033: We build the Guider based on the diffusion process to express multimodal contact distributions, thereby achieving accurate high-level planning.
elements.sent-2411-034: Hierarchical planning makes the internal logic of the trajectory generated at each step more transparent.
elements.sent-2411-035: Thanks to the precise guidance of robot actions towards objective contact, the robot's operational intent becomes apparent well in advance.
elements.sent-2411-036: We can intervene in the operation process at any time and guide the robot to complete the task in the desired way by designating the objective contact positions, which enhances the human-machine interaction.
elements.sent-2411-037: To further unlock the potential of HDP, this paper proposes several key technical contributions.
elements.sent-2411-039: Wang et al. optimize the diffusion-based policy by calculating the gradient of the policy at all timesteps along the diffusion process in the Q-learning algorithm, which resulted in inefficient training \cite{wang2022diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-040: We propose the snapshot gradient optimization strategy that calculates the gradient of only one timestep in the diffusion process per iteration, significantly improving training speed.
elements.sent-2411-041: To improve the perception of the policy, we introduce 3D point clouds to represent the state.
elements.sent-2411-042: The policy extracts the 3D representation once regardless of the denoising iteration.
elements.sent-2411-043: Manually designating objective contacts without using the Guider can produce customized operation trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-044: This demonstrates the potential application of HDP in enhancing human-machine interaction.
elements.sent-2411-045: We systematically evaluate HDP across 6 tasks.
elements.sent-2411-046: The evaluation includes both simulated and real-world environments, 2DOF to 6DOF actions, prehensile and non-prehensile operations, with rigid and deformable objects, using demonstration data collected by single and multiple users.
elements.sent-2411-047: Empirically, we find significant performance boost with an average improvement of 20.8\%, providing strong evidence of the effectiveness of HDP.
elements.sent-2411-048: We also provide detailed analysis to carefully examine the characteristics of the proposed algorithm and the impacts of the key design decisions.
elements.sent-2411-049: The code, data, and training details will be publicly available.
elements.sent-2411-050: Long-horizon tasks have been a hot topic in the field of robotics in recent years due to their complexity and sparse rewards \cite{guo2023recent}\cite{orthey2023sampling .}
elements.sent-2411-051: An intuitive approach is to hierarchically decompose long-horizon tasks to reduce task complexity \cite{triantafyllidis2023hybrid .}
elements.sent-2411-052: From a learning perspective, long-horizon tasks are difficult to learn using reinforcement learning algorithms due to sparse rewards, resulting in very low training efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-053: Additionally, frequent interactions can increase the safety risks for the robot \cite{singh2022reinforcement .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Current research trends are gradually shifting towards learning manipulation policies from pre-collected demonstrations \cite{zheng2024imitation}\cite{prudencio2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Below we briefly review related work on hierarchical manipulation and learning from demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-056: Non-learning methods usually decompose the complete state space and perform continuous planning separately.
elements.sent-2411-057: A representative approach first searches for the object's movement trajectory in space, then searches for the robot's operational trajectory, and finally optimizes the results \cite{aceituno2022hierarchical}\cite{cheng2023enhancing .}
elements.sent-2411-058: This type of method is stable but limited to objects and scenes with known models, resulting in insufficient generalization.
elements.sent-2411-059: Most learning-based methods are categorized by the scope of high-level actions into task-specific, finite set, LLM-based planning, fixed-step planning, and object motion-related planning.
elements.sent-2411-060: Task-specific methods limit high-level actions to specific tasks related to the task, and are highly efficient in learning but cannot be used for other tasks \cite{simonivc2024hierarchical}\cite{9745587}\cite{luo2024multi .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Finite set-based methods collect reusable skills from demonstrations as high-level actions, which are also highly efficient in learning but can only be used for a few tasks composed of skills \cite{zhu2022bottom}\cite{yang2021hierarchical}\cite{rana2023residual .}
elements.sent-2411-062: With the rapid development of LLMs, some methods use the commonsense knowledge of LLMs for task planning.
elements.sent-2411-063: While they can handle most everyday tasks, they struggle with tasks requiring precise manipulation \cite{ajay2024compositional}\cite{jia2023chain}\cite{brohan2023can .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Hierarchical reinforcement learning methods, which predict high-level actions with fixed steps, are widely used in various types of robots, but they do not focus on the objects to be manipulated \cite{hafner2022deep}\cite{zhang2022adjacency}\cite{huang2022planning .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Ma et al. decompose the prehensile task into predicting the grasping posture and predicting the robot trajectory, which effectively improved the success rate \cite{ma2024hierarchical .}
elements.sent-2411-066: In contrast, our previous work \cite{wang2024multi use contact as a subgoal of the low-level policy, which can be easily extended to different end-effectors (such as dexterous hands and single-finger grippers) rather than being limited to parallel-jaw grippers.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Additionally, it can be applied to non-prehensile tasks (such as flipping and tilting).
elements.sent-2411-068: In this paper, we improve the contact planning in our previous work \cite{wang2024multi from being computed based on an object model to being generated by a denoised diffusion process to improve generalization.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The simplest form of learning from demonstration is behavioral cloning, which is copying the behavioral policy of collecting demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-070: Current behavior cloning approaches can be categorized into two groups, depending on the policy's structure.
elements.sent-2411-071: Explicit policies directly map observations to actions \cite{zhang2018deep}\cite{florence2019self}\cite{toyer2020magical .}
elements.sent-2411-072: To model multimodal action distributions, some methods model the mapping as a classification problem instead of a regression problem \cite{zeng2021transporter}\cite{avigal2022speedfolding .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Further approaches combine categorical and Gaussian distributions to represent continuous multimodal distributions \cite{mandlekar2022matters .}
elements.sent-2411-074: However, these models often face challenges such as difficult hyperparameter tuning, model collapse, and limited expression \cite{florence2022implicit .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Implicit policies use energy-based models to model the action distribution, which can effectively express multimodal distribution \cite{du2021improved}\cite{grathwohl2020learning .}
elements.sent-2411-076: However, existing implicit policies \cite{florence2022implicit are unstable to train due to the necessity of drawing negative samples.}
elements.sent-2411-077: One of the main issues with behavior cloning is its inability to learn policies that surpass the behaviors demonstrated in the collected data.
elements.sent-2411-078: A solution to this problem is offline reinforcement learning, which optimizes policies through value functions, allowing for better handling of suboptimal data and adaptation to unseen states \cite{zhan2022offline}\cite{cheng2022adversarially}\cite{luo2023action .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Unlike online reinforcement learning, offline reinforcement learning cannot correct erroneous behaviors by interacting with the environment, so it is necessary to find a balance between increased generalization and avoiding unwanted behaviors outside of distribution, namely distributional shift \cite{prudencio2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-080: Many researchers have proposed different solutions to distribution shifts, including policy constraints \cite{fujimoto2019off}\cite{nair2020awac , importance sampling \cite{precup2000eligibility}\cite{zhanggendice , regularization \cite{haarnoja2018soft}\cite{kumar2020conservative , uncertainty estimation \cite{agarwal2020optimistic , etc. We combine offline reinforcement learning with behavior cloning, and the optimization objectives include minimizing the action difference and maximizing the value function, corresponding to the above-mentioned policy constraints.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: The diffusion model, also known as the denoised diffusion probability model, generates data that conforms to a specific distribution through an iterative denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-082: They have demonstrated amazing generative capabilities on both unconditional and conditional image, video, and 3D object generation \cite{rombach2022high}\cite{ho2022cascaded}\cite{dhariwal2021diffusion}\cite{wu2023tune}\cite{liu2024sora}\cite{vahdat2022lion}\cite{luo2021diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-083: In the decision-making domain, diffusion models have also recently been used to model multimodal action distributions \cite{song2020denoising}\cite{nichol2021improved}\cite{rombach2022high}\cite{black2023training}\cite{ajayconditional .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Among them, Diffusion Policy constructs a diffusion-based robot trajectory generation policy with behavior cloning \cite{chi2023diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Some researchers have enhanced Diffusion Policy from various aspects, such as state reconstruction \cite{li2023crossway and hierarchical policy \cite{ma2024hierarchical .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Concurrent with our work, Ma et al. proposed to first predict the robot's end pose and then predict the action sequence ending at the target pose through a diffusion-based policy with behavior cloning \cite{ma2024hierarchical .}
elements.sent-2411-087: However, due to the limitations of behavior cloning, they still face the same issues as Diffusion Policy, namely the difficulty in learning policies beyond the behavior policy and the accumulation of errors.
elements.sent-2411-088: In addition, pose is not sufficient to express the states of different end-effectors, such as dexterous hands with more contact points.
elements.sent-2411-089: In contrast, our method introduces an additional value function that enables the policy to focus on longer time steps rather than just the current state, allowing for better handling of suboptimal data and adaptation to unseen states.
elements.sent-2411-090: Additionally, we use contact states as high-level actions, which makes it easier to apply to end-effectors with different numbers of fingers.
elements.sent-2411-091: We formulate HDP as Conditional Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (CDDPMs) \cite{ho2022classifier .}
elements.sent-2411-092: HDP is able to express multimodal contact distributions and predict action sequences that are also multimodal based on contact conditions.
elements.sent-2411-093: Crucially, HDP can ensure the controllability of trajectory generation.
elements.sent-2411-094: The following sections describe CDDPMs in more detail and explain how construct HDP.
elements.sent-2411-095: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) are a type of generative model that generates high-quality data samples by gradually denoising \cite{nichol2021improved .}
elements.sent-2411-096: It includes a forward process and a reverse process.
elements.sent-2411-097: The forward process begins with the action data $x_0$ and defines a Markov chain that progressively adds noise over $K$ steps: \begin{equation}q(x^k|x^{k-1}) = N(x^k; \sqrt{\alpha^k} x^{k-1}, (1-\alpha^k)I) \label{eq_31}\end{equation} where $\alpha^k$ is the noise scheduling parameter at timestep $k$.
elements.sent-2411-098: The iterative noise addition process can be combined into the following formula: \begin{equation}x^k = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}^k} x^0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}^k} z^k \label{eq_32}\end{equation} where $\bar{\alpha ^k=\begin{matrix \prod_{i=1 ^K \alpha_i \end{matrix}$, $z^k$ is the random normally distributed noise at step $k$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: The reverse process starts with $x^K$ sampled from Gaussian noise, the DDPM undergoes $K$ iterations of denoising, generating a sequence of intermediate actions with progressively reduced noise levels, until achieving the desired noise-free output $x^0$.
elements.sent-2411-100: The process follows the equation: \begin{equation}p_{\theta}(x^{k-1}|x^k, y) = \notag N( x^{k-1}; \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha^k}}(x^k-\frac{1-\alpha^k}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}^k}} \epsilon_{\theta}(x^k, k, y) ), (1-\alpha^k) I ) \label{eq_33}\end{equation} where $y$ represents the conditional information considered during data generation.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}p_{\theta}(x^{k-1}|x^k, y) = \notag N( x^{k-1}; \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha^k}}(x^k-\frac{1-\alpha^k}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}^k}} \epsilon_{\theta}(x^k, k, y) ), (1-\alpha^k) I ) \label{eq_33}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-101: By transforming Eq.~\ref{eq_32 , $x^0$ can be calculated directly from $x^k$ without the need for iterative reverse diffusion, as follows: \begin{equation}x^0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}^k}}(x^k - \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}^k} z^k) \label{eq_34}\end{equation}.}
elements.figure-2411-002: {\includegraphics[scale=0.45]{overview/overview.pdf}} \caption{ Hierarchical Diffusion Policy Overview. (a) At time step $t$ during inference, the Guider takes the latest $T_o$ steps of observation data $\mathbf{O_t}$ as input and predicts objective contact $\mathbf{C_t}$, the Actor takes observation data $\mathbf{O_t}$ and objective contact $\mathbf{C_t}$ as input and predicts $T_p$ steps of actions, of which $T_a$ steps of actions are executed on the robot without re-planning. During training, in addition to minimizing the prediction error of the Actor, Guider, and Critic compared to the ground truth, the Actor's weights are also optimized by maximizing the Q-values. (b) The Actor and Guider are modeled as conditional denoising diffusion models, with their networks built on one-dimensional convolutions and linear layers, respectively. The Actor's architecture is similar to that of the Diffusion Policy. Critic is built based on linear layers. } \label{fig_overview}

elements.sent-2411-134: At each time step during inference, the Guider first inputs the observation to predict the objective contact, and then the Actor inputs the observation and contact to predict the trajectory.
elements.figure-2411-003: {\includegraphics[scale=0.36]{oc/oc.pdf}} \caption{ Phased objective contacts. The algorithm consists of three steps: recording object subgoals and contacts, merging misoperations and no-contact operations, and configuring objective contacts. Misoperations include \textcircled{1} overshooting the movement and \textcircled{2} not moving or barely moving the object. The algorithm eliminates contacts related to misoperations by detecting object pose similarity, reducing suboptimal objective contacts (last row). Additionally, this process also removes the contacts related to operations near subgoals, resulting in an early update of the objective contact (the end of the last row), which is experimentally proven not to impair performance (Fig.~\ref{fig_result_oc}). } \label{fig_oc}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Phased objective contacts} \label{alg1} \KwIn{demonstration $D$ of length $N$, maximum reward $R$, Q-learning action steps $T_r$} \KwOut{objective contact $C_t$, $C'_t$ and reward $R_t$ for all $t \in [0, N-T_r]$} \SetKwFunction{FNA}{RecordFunction} \SetKwFunction{FNB}{MergeFunction} \SetKwFunction{FNC}{ConfigureFunction} // Step 1. Record the original contact status and object pose subgoal $C_o, P, P_I = $ \FNA{$D$}; // Step 2. Merge contacts of no moving objects $C_o, P, P_I = $ \FNB{$C_o$, $P$, $P_I$}; // Step 3. Configure objective contacts and rewards $C, C', R = $ \FNC{D, $C_o$, $P$, $P_I$, $R$}; \Return{$C\leftarrow[C_0, ..., C_{N-T_r}]$, $C'\leftarrow[C'_0, ..., C'_{N-T_r}]$, $R\leftarrow[R_0, ..., R_{N-T_r}]$}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{RecordFunction} \label{RecordFunction} \KwIn{demonstration $D$ of length $N$} \KwOut{original contact status $C_o$, object pose subgoal $P$ and corresponding time steps $P_I$} $c_f \leftarrow false$; // Mark whether the robot is in contact with the object $C_o = P = P_I = \emptyset$; \For{$i=0$ to $N$}{ update $c_f$, contact position $c_p$, object pose $p$; $C_o = C_o \cup (c_f, c_p)$; \If{$c_f$ changes}{ $P = P \cup p$, $P_I = P_I \cup i$; } } \Return{$C_o$, $P$, $P_I$}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{MergeFunction} \label{MergeFunction} \KwIn{original contact status $C_o$, object pose subgoal $P$ and corresponding time steps $P_I$} \KwOut{merged contact status $C_o$, object pose subgoal $P$ and corresponding time steps $P_I$} \While{$i<|P|$}{ calculate similarity $\rho$ of $P[i]$ and $P[i+1]$ according to Eq.~\ref{eq_411}; \eIf{$\rho > 0$}{ \For{$j=P_I[i]$ to $P_I[i+1]$}{ $C_o[j] \leftarrow (0, 0)$; } del $P[i], P_I[i]$; }{ $i \leftarrow i+1$; } } del $C_o[0]$; $C_o = C_o \cup (0, 0)$; // Take the contact of the next time as the objective of the current time step \Return{$C_o$, $P$, $P_I$}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{ConfigureFunction} \label{ConfigureFunction} \KwIn{demonstration $D$ of length $N$, merged contact status $C_o$, object pose subgoal $P$ and corresponding time steps $P_I$, maximum reward $R$, distance thresh $\tau_f$.} \KwOut{objective contact $C_t$, $C'_t$ and reward $R_t$ for all $t \in [0, N-T_r]$} $C = C' = R = \emptyset$; $p' \leftarrow 0$; // The serial number of the arrival object pose subgoal $d \leftarrow true$; // Mark whether the object reaches the subgoal $r \leftarrow 0$; // Reward \For{$i=0$ to $N-T_r$}{ \If{$d = true$ and $r = R$}{ $d = false$; // Update marker when robot reaches objective contact } \If{$d = false$}{ calculate similarity $\rho$ of $p$ and $P[p'+1]$ according to Eq.~\ref{eq_411}; \If{$\rho > 0$}{ $p' \leftarrow p'+1$; $d = true$; // Update marker when object reaches the subgoal } } // Configure objective contact $i' = max(P_I[p'], i)$; $C = C \cup C_I[i']$; $c' \leftarrow$ objective contact at time $i+T_r$ and its position relative to the object is the same as $C_I[i']$; $C' = C' \cup c'$; // Calculate reward \For{$n=1$ to $T_r$}{ $c \leftarrow$ objective contact at time $i+n$ and its position relative to the object is the same as $C_I[i']$; $f \leftarrow$ finger position; \eIf{$|f-c| < \tau_f$}{ $r \leftarrow R$; break; }{ $r \leftarrow 0$; } } $R = R \cup r$; } \Return{$C, C', R$}
elements.sent-2411-135: In this section we outline the key design decisions for Hierarchical Diffusion Policy.
elements.sent-2411-136: The first design decision is the choice of Guider's generation target, i.e., objective contact.
elements.sent-2411-137: The following misoperations usually occur in demonstrations: (1) the robot accidentally touches the object without moving it, and (2) does not stop properly after moving the object to the goal state, but instead corrects itself after the misoperation to complete the task.
elements.sent-2411-138: Taking the immediate contact as the objective would cause the policy to reproduce these misoperations during inference.
elements.sent-2411-139: We observe that the above misoperations have something in common, that is, they do not change the state of the object.
elements.sent-2411-140: Removing objective contacts that do not change the object's state may alleviate misoperations during inference.
elements.sent-2411-141: Based on this assumption, we design a novel method to construct phased objective contacts that exclude misoperations (Algorithm \ref{alg1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-142: The algorithm flow is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig_oc .}
elements.sent-2411-143: Specifically, the algorithm first divides the manipulation process into multiple phases according to the changes in contact state, and the object pose at the last time step of each phase is recorded as a subgoal (Algorithm \ref{RecordFunction ).}
elements.sent-2411-144: The changed contact refers to switching from touching to detaching or vice versa, independent of changes in the contact position.
elements.sent-2411-145: Then the algorithm merges adjacent phases with similar subgoals and removes the subgoal from the previous phase and contacts within the next phase (Algorithm \ref{MergeFunction ).}
elements.sent-2411-146: This process not only merges misoperations but also combines the phase where the object has never been touched with the previous phase.
elements.sent-2411-147: At the time step with contact, the objective contact is set to the position of the finger with contact.
elements.sent-2411-148: Once the object reaches the subgoal, the objective contact is set to the contact position of the first step in the next stage, even if the current stage has not ended due to an misoperation (Algorithm \ref{ConfigureFunction ).}
elements.sent-2411-149: According to Eq.~\ref{eq_49 , the misoperation requires more time steps to reach the objective contact, resulting in a smaller Q-value.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Therefore, optimizing the policy by maximizing the Q-value can avoid generating misoperations and improve efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-151: In Algorithm \ref{alg1 , object pose similarity $\rho$ is calculated according to the following formula: \begin{equation}\rho =  1, if dl < \tau_l and dr < \tau_r 0, else  \label{eq_411}\end{equation} where $dl$ and $dr$ are the Euclidean distance and angle between two object poses respectively, $\tau_l$ and $\tau_r$ are the distance and angle thresholds respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-152: The 3D encoder maps the raw object point clouds into a latent embedding $O_t$ and is trained end-to-end with the HDP.
elements.sent-2411-153: For rigid objects, point clouds are derived from the model and transformed into the world coordinate system according to the pose.
elements.sent-2411-154: For deformable objects, point clouds are captured by stereo cameras or consist of keypoints.
elements.sent-2411-155: Point clouds in each timestep are concatenated and then encoded to form $O_t$.
elements.sent-2411-156: We modify a standard PointNet \cite{qi2017pointnet and use it as the encoder.}
elements.sent-2411-157: The modifications include: (1) removing input and feature transformations to make the network sensitive to object pose; (2) removing all normalization processing to make the network sensitive to object size.
elements.sent-2411-158: This is crucial because the object's pose and size directly influence how operations are carried out.
elements.sent-2411-159: The comparison of our new 3D encoder with PointNet and PointNet++ \cite{qi2017pointnet++ is shown in Tab.~\ref{tab_encoders .}}
elements.sent-2411-160: Our 3D encoder outperforms others in both performance and inference speed.
elements.sent-2411-161: The performance of HDP using PointNet and PointNet++ with normalization is even worse than without using a 3D encoder, indicating that normalization not only prevents the network from accurately perceiving object information but also interferes with the network's processing of other observation.

A larger coefficient leads to greater optimization intensity and more optimization conflicts. The sensitivity of HDP to coefficient changes and gradient imbalance caused by iterative denoising increases as the number of training samples decreases. } \label{fig_eta_reverse}
elements.sent-2411-162: In the data tuples $(O_t, C_t, A_t, r, O_{t+T_r , C'_{t+T_r , A_{t+T_r )$ from the demonstration, $A_t$ is either suboptimal or optimal.}}}
elements.sent-2411-163: The Critic trained on this data based on Eq.~\ref{eq_49 will only output similar Q values even for poor trajectories, thus failing to optimize the actor to generate the optimal trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-164: We alleviate this problem by adding noise to $A_t$ to expand the data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-165: Specifically, the added noise consists of two levels: minor and major.
elements.sent-2411-166: The minor noise slightly changes $A_t$ so that the observation after $T_r$ step is almost consistent with $O_{t+T_r}$.
elements.sent-2411-167: Conversely, major noise drastically changes the $A_t$ and the observation after $T_r$ step.
elements.sent-2411-168: The action after adding noise is formulated as: \begin{equation}\tilde{A}_t = P (A_t + max(\tau_{n1}, min(\tau_{n2}, N(0, \mu_1I)))) \notag + (1-P)(A_t + N(0, \mu_2I)) \label{eq_431}\end{equation} where $P$ takes $1$ or $0$ with the same probability, corresponding to minor and major noise respectively.
elements.sent-2411-169: The rewards corresponding to different noises are as follows: \begin{equation}r =  r, if P = 1 0, else  \label{eq_432}\end{equation} We set the target Q-value of the trajectories with major noise to $0$ to suppress the motion patterns that significantly deviate from the demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-170: Eq.~\ref{eq_49 is simplified to the following if $P=0$: \begin{equation}L_c = MSE(0, Q_{\phi}(O_t, C_t, \tilde{A}_t) \label{eq_433}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-171: In the actor loss function established in Eq.~\ref{eq_45 , the design decisions related to Q-learning include the setting of the coefficient $\eta$ and the denoising formula of $A^0_{t,\theta}$.}
elements.sent-2411-172: A comprehensive comparison of different coefficients $\eta$ and denoising formulas is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_eta_reverse .}
elements.sent-2411-173: We find that HDP with the oneshot denoising formula consistently outperforms HDP with iterative denoising.
elements.sent-2411-174: This surprising result stands in contrast to the majority of recent diffusion reinforcement learning work that generally relies on iterative denoising \cite{wang2022diffusion, ada2024diffusion, black2023training .}
elements.sent-2411-175: We speculate that the main reason for this performance difference is that the network weights require different optimization intensities at different time steps.
elements.sent-2411-176: The data at the earlier inverse diffusion time steps contains more noise components, so the network needs to learn stronger denoising ability, resulting in higher optimization intensity and larger gradient.
elements.sent-2411-177: Iterative denoising does not balance the gradient of each step when jointly optimizing the weights, which weakens the optimization intensity at smaller time steps and causes the generated trajectory to deviate from the target distribution.
elements.sent-2411-178: In contrast, oneshot denoising ensures the best optimization intensity by individually optimizing the weights at each time step.
elements.sent-2411-179: Additionally, oneshot denoising increased the training speed by 4 times and reduced the GPU memory usage by 24\% when the denoising step is set to 10.
elements.sent-2411-180: We name the method of optimizing the Actor through oneshot denoising as snapshot gradient optimization.
elements.sent-2411-181: The experiment also verifies that a coefficient greater than 0 can help the policy reduce misoperations, but a coefficient that is too large will make the optimization intensity of Q-learning too high and conflict with imitation learning.
elements.sent-2411-182: We find that the coefficient of $0.001$ is optimal for most of the tasks we tested.
elements.sent-2411-183: With fewer demonstrations, the improvement from oneshot denoising is greater, and the policy is more sensitive to changes in the coefficient.

elements.sent-2411-184: In this section, we provide some insights and intuitions about hierarchical diffusion policy and its advantages over other forms of policy representations.
elements.sent-2411-185: The challenges of modeling multi-modal distributions in demonstrations have been widely discussed in the literature \cite{florence2022implicit}\cite{ shafiullah2022behavior}\cite{mandlekar2022matters .}
elements.sent-2411-186: The key advantage of HDP is that it decomposes the problem of directly modeling multi-modal action distributions into two sub-problems: modeling the objective contact and the action with simpler distributions, which effectively reduces the modeling complexity and improves accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-187: In previous end-to-end modeling methods $f$, for a given observation $O$, the action distribution $\mathcal{A}$ typically includes multiple different contact strategies, expressed as follows: \begin{equation}f(O) = A \notag subject to \quad C_A = \{C_A|A \in A \} = \{ C_1, C_2, C_3, ... \} \label{eq_511}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{C _\mathcal{A}$ is the set of contact strategies included in the action distribution $\mathcal{A}$.}
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}f(O) = A \notag subject to \quad C_A = \{C_A|A \in A \} = \{ C_1, C_2, C_3, ... \} \label{eq_511}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-188: In contrast, due to the accurate guidance of Q-learning on the generated trajectories, the action distribution $\bar{\mathcal{A }$ of HDP conditioned on the observation $O$ and objective contact $C$ only contains the given contact strategy: \begin{equation}f(O, C) = \bar{A} \notag subject to \quad C_{\bar{A}} = \{C_{\bar{A}}|\bar{A} \in {\bar{A}} \} = \{C\} \label{eq_512}\end{equation} Fewer contact strategies lead to a simpler multimodal distribution, reducing complexity and resulting in more stable predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Therefore, under the same policy modeling capabilities, HDP models the action distribution more accurately.
elements.sent-2411-190: Additionally, the powerful expressive capability of the diffusion model ensures the accuracy of modeling the objective contact $C$.
elements.sent-2411-191: Fig.~\ref{fig_decompose shows an example of the HDP's multimodal behavior in a object reorientation task (Move-T, introduced below).}
elements.sent-2411-192: Thanks to HDP's accurate objective contact prediction and trajectory guidance, the robot can successfully reach the appropriate operating position every time.
elements.sent-2411-193: With the rapid development of large language models, many methods use language models to guide the generation of robot motion trajectories, but most of them are limited to approximate spatial positions \cite{huang2023voxposer}\cite{driess2023palm}\cite{10549793 .}
elements.sent-2411-194: Another key advantage of HDP is that humans can accurately guide the robot's trajectory by designating objective contacts even outside the training distribution.
elements.sent-2411-195: This ability stems from the fact that Q-learning suppresses trajectories that deviate from target contact.
elements.sent-2411-196: Thanks to the joint optimization of Q-learning and imitation learning, the trajectory does not go straight to the target contact, but remains close to the demonstrated behavior pattern.
elements.sent-2411-197: We name a manually designated objective contact as a prompt.
elements.sent-2411-198: Fig.~\ref{fig_control shows an example of the HDP's prompt guidance in a planar pushing task (Push-T, introduced below).}
elements.sent-2411-199: When prompts conform to the demonstrated trajectory distribution, HDP can generate effective operation trajectories that pass through each prompt in sequence.
elements.sent-2411-200: Although the objective contacts used for training are all located on the surface of objects, prompts outside the training distribution and far from the objects can still effectively guide the robot's motion.
elements.sent-2411-201: As the prompt gradually deviates from the demonstrated trajectory distribution, HDP experiences a conflict between following the prompt and performing the task as demonstrated.
elements.sent-2411-202: In contrast, Diffusion Policy \cite{chi2023diffusion exhibits consistent movement patterns in the same test scenario and could not be intervened by humans.}
elements.sent-2411-203: Prompt guidance enables the policy to cope with changes in task requirements without retraining, for example, constraining the range of motion of the end-effector in Push-T task.
elements.sent-2411-204: In addition, prompt guidance enhances HDP's potential to improve human-machine interaction, for example, correcting failed operations in rehearsed trajectories.

Compared with no-OC scheme, contact guidance improves the stability of the results. Compared with nearest-OC scheme, the phased-OC scheme improves the success rate by filtering out misoperations. } \label{fig_result_oc}
elements.sent-2411-205: We test the performance of different objective contact schemes (Fig.~\ref{fig_result_oc ) in three tasks, and find two phenomena: (1) phased objective contact scheme has consistent advantages; (2) policies with objective contact guidance are more stable in results than policies without, that is, the difference between the average success rate and the maximum success rate is smaller.}
elements.sent-2411-206: The nearest objective contact scheme takes the immediate contact as the objective.
elements.sent-2411-207: As described in Sec.~\ref{sec_oc and Sec.~\ref{sec_q_learning , the performance improvement of the phased objective contact scheme comes from filtering misoperations and accurate guidance.}}
elements.sent-2411-208: We attribute the improvement in stability to HDP being guided by contact, which aligns the generated trajectories more closely with the task objectives, rather than merely following the trajectory distribution learned during imitation learning.
elements.sent-2411-209: This alignment enhances the consistency of the trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-210: The results in Fig.~\ref{fig_control verify this conjecture, that is, when there is a prompt located to the right of the object, the consistency of HDP's trajectories is better than that of DP.}
elements.subsection-2411-013: Effective for Any Q-Learning Action Steps
elements.sent-2411-211: We find that HDP is not sensitive to the number of Q-learning action steps (Fig.~\ref{fig_T_r ).}
elements.sent-2411-212: This surprising result stands in contrast to the majority of recent reinforcement learning and trajectory generation work that is sensitive to the number of action steps \cite{kumar2023sample}\cite{wang2024multi}\cite{ze20243d .}
elements.sent-2411-213: We speculate that the reason for this discrepancy lies in the smoothness of the demonstration trajectories and the data continuity of the convolutional diffusion network.
elements.sent-2411-214: Even if Q-learning only optimizes the first $T_r$ actions, the subsequent actions generated by HDP (starting from step $T_r+1$) can still maintain continuity and smoothness.

elements.sent-2411-215: In this paper, we focus on testing the performance of HDP on tasks with single robot and single object.
elements.sent-2411-216: We systematically evaluate HDP on 6 tasks.
elements.sent-2411-217: This evaluation suite includes both simulated and real environments, single and multiple task benchmarks, single and multiple operation modes, and rigid and deformable objects.
elements.sent-2411-218: We find HDP to significantly outperform the prior state-of-the-art on all of the tested benchmarks, with an average success-rate improvement of 20.8\%.
elements.sent-2411-219: In the following sections, we provide an overview of each task, our evaluation methodology on that task, and our key takeaways.

elements.sent-2411-220: We choose 2 tasks in the benchmark with a proficient human (PH) teleoperated demonstration dataset and mixed proficient/non-proficient human (MH) demonstration datasets for each (4 variants in total).

elements.sent-2411-240: Following paragraphs summarize the key takeaways.
elements.sent-2411-241: HDP can express short-horizon and long-horizon multimodality.

elements.sent-2411-244: Long-horizon action multimodality refers to the various ways of completing a task along different contact sequences.
elements.sent-2411-245: Fig.~\ref{fig_decompose shows that HDP learns to complete the Move-T task with two different target contact sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-246: Detailed experiments of HDP on the Move-T task are in Sec.~ \ref{sec_moveT .}
elements.sent-2411-247: In all tasks, HDP with no-OC achieves improvements.
elements.sent-2411-248: We find that the improvement is greater in the following three cases: (1) fewer training samples, (2) complex object shapes (e.g., Square), and (3) variable object shapes (e.g., Tilt).
elements.sent-2411-249: HDP is better at learning optimal demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-250: In tasks requiring precise manipulation, the manually collected demonstration dataset contains many inadvertent touches that change the object's position which cannot be removed by Algorithm~\ref{alg1 , causing the Guider to learn objective contacts that reproduce these mistakes (e.g., Square task).}

elements.sent-2411-255: The realworld Move-T task is an extension of the simulated Push-T task.
elements.sent-2411-256: The extensions mainly include the following: (1) the end-effector is a commonly used parallel gripper instead of a cylinder; (2) the operation modes include both push and grasp to fully utilize the gripper's capabilities; (3) the task is multi-stage, and the robot needs to first move the T block to the target position and then move the gripper into a designated end-zone; (4) the IoU metric is measured at the last step instead of taking the maximum over all steps.
elements.sent-2411-257: Our UR5e-based experiment setup is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_moveT_setup .}
elements.sent-2411-258: Each demonstration in the dataset contains only one operation mode of pushing or grasping.
elements.sent-2411-259: Action dimensions include 3D translation, rotation along the vertical axis, and manipulator closure.
elements.sent-2411-260: The input observations are object poses and point clouds as well as the robot's proprioception.
elements.sent-2411-261: The point cloud is generated by transforming the pre-constructed T block point cloud to the object positions obtained through Iterative Closest Point (ICP) alignment.
elements.sent-2411-262: For the Move-T task, Algorithm~\ref{RecordFunction of HDP additionally records the gripper finger positions in the last step of the demonstration as objective contacts to provide guidance for the operation in stage 2.}
elements.sent-2411-263: The action sequence predicted by HDP runs at 5 Hz.

elements.sent-2411-300: Although HDP has demonstrated effective learning and generalization capabilities in the selected tasks, it still has some limitations and room for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-301: Firstly, the prompt guidance experiment in Fig.~\ref{fig_control on the Push-T task shows that HDP struggles to generate effective trajectories towards the prompt when it does not align with the distribution of demonstrated trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-302: We speculate that the reason for the limited guidance is that the lack of demonstration data makes HDP unable to generate effective data for unlearned conditions.
elements.sent-2411-303: We will try to improve the controllability of HDP by training it on larger datasets, and explore ways to improve its generalization with limited datasets.
elements.sent-2411-304: Secondly, since objective contact guides the output of the Actor, the prediction accuracy of Guider significantly influences the quality of HDP's final outputs.
elements.sent-2411-305: Considering the impressive performance of Visual Language Models (VLMs) in recent years in fields such as logical reasoning and spatial modeling, modeling objective contacts using VLMs is expected to enhance Guider's generalization and prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-306: Last, the general formulation of HDP allows it to scale across multiple robots and end-effectors with varying numbers of contact points.
elements.sent-2411-307: We will validate HDP's performance in operational tasks involving multi-robot and multi-finger end-effectors in future work.
elements.sent-2411-308: In this paper, we propose a manipulation trajectory generation method called Hierarchical Diffusion Policy (HDP) which combines behavior cloning and Q-learning strategy for hierarchical learning.
elements.sent-2411-309: Through comprehensive evaluation on four simulated tasks and two real-world tasks, we show that HDP significantly outperforms state-of-the-art imitation learning methods and exhibits greater interpretability and controllability, previously unseen in similar methods, which enables HDP to receive human prompts to generate customized trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-310: Our results also validate some key design choices, including phased objective contacts, 3D vision encoders, and snapshot gradient optimization, that are critical to unlocking the full potential of HDP.
elements.sent-2411-311: Furthermore, results in real-world scenarios validate that HDP can rely on human prompts to overcome local optima traps and reliably complete tasks under various visual and physical interferences.
elements.sent-2411-312: However, the controllability and generalization of HDP are still limited by the scale of training data and model capacity, which will be further explored in our future work.

elements.sent-2411-322: To ensure training stability, we use exponential moving average (EMA) to smooth the weights of the Actor and Guider, as follows: \begin{equation}\theta' = (1-(1+s)^{-\sigma})\theta' + (1+s)^{-\sigma}\theta\end{equation} where $\theta'$ is the smoothed network weight, $s$ is the number of training iterations, and $\sigma$ is used to calculate the smoothing factor.
elements.sent-2411-323: The weights of the target critic $\phi'$ are obtained by calculating the EMA of the critic weights $\phi$, as follows: \begin{equation}\phi' = (1-\sigma)\phi' + \sigma\phi\end{equation} We maintain two target critic networks concurrently and select the minimum output to compute the target Q-value (Eq.~\ref{eq_49 ), enhancing training stability and convergence speed.}
